coredata.dc:title: Antibacterial property of dual-network hydrogel filled with amino functionalized graphene quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infections seriously impair wound healing, so the development of wound dressings with high antimicrobial activity is of critical importance. In this study, amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) were prepared via thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and histidine, and doped into a dual-network gelatin/sodium alginate (His-GQDs/G/SA) hydrogel to improve the antibacterial activity. The His-GQDs/G/SA hydrogel exhibited excellent antibacterial activity after 6 h of contact with bacteria. Simultaneously, the composite hydrogel could effectively alleviate the cytotoxicity of His-GQDs. The results of antimicrobial and biocompatibility tests demonstrate that the His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel is a promising wound dressing to promote healing of infected wounds.

Graphene quantum dots
Dual-network hydrogel
Antimicrobial activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HR-TEM images of His-GQDs; (c) FTIR spectrum of His-GQDs; (d)XPS full survey spectrum of His-GQDs; high-resolution XPS spectra of (e) C 1 s, (f) N 1 s and (g) O 1 s. (h) SEM image of His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel;

(a) Photographs of S. aureus and (b) Antibacterial efficiency against S. aureus; (c) SEM images of S. aureus after treated with different samples; (d) Live/dead fluorescent staining. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001, n = 3).
(a) Live/dead staining of osteoblasts after incubation for 1, 3 and 5 days; (b) Cytoskeleton of osteoblasts after incubation for for 24 h.

head.ce:title: Antibacterial property of dual-network hydrogel filled with amino functionalized graphene quantum dots

College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infections seriously impair wound healing, so the development of wound dressings with high antimicrobial activity is of critical importance. In this study, amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) were prepared via thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and histidine, and doped into a dual-network gelatin/sodium alginate (His-GQDs/G/SA) hydrogel to improve the antibacterial activity. The His-GQDs/G/SA hydrogel exhibited excellent antibacterial activity after 6 h of contact with bacteria. Simultaneously, the composite hydrogel could effectively alleviate the cytotoxicity of His-GQDs. The results of antimicrobial and biocompatibility tests demonstrate that the His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel is a promising wound dressing to promote healing of infected wounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The infection of damaged skin areas caused by bacteria is a major obstacle to wound healing [1]. Fortunately, carbon-based nanomaterials have been proven effective in the treatment of bacterial infections [2]. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a 0-dimensional graphene-based material extensively used in antimicrobial applications due to its rapid bactericidal capability [3,4]. However, the potential toxicity of GQDs limits the application of GQDs in biological fields [5].

The gelatin (G) is derived from degraded collagen and has the advantages of good biodegradability, excellent biocompatibility and non-immunogenicity [6]. The sodium alginate (SA) is a natural non-toxic polysaccharide with advantages of cheap, non-toxicity and good biocompatibility [7]. The dual-network G/SA hydrogel formed by cross-linking has good mechanical properties, and can provide a moist environment which is beneficial to wound healing [8]. Nevertheless, G/SA hydrogel lacks antimicrobial properties to prevent wound infections [9].

In this work, histidine functionalized GQDs (His-GQDs) were doped into G/SA to prepare His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network composite hydrogel by using calcium ion as a cross-linking agent. His-GQDs were able to provide His-GQDs/G/SA hydrogel with good antimicrobial activity, while His-GQDs/G/SA hydrogel could reduce the biotoxicity of the His-GQDs.

His-GQDs were synthesized according to the procedures described previously [10]. The detailed preparation process of His-GQDs and His-GQDs/G/SA dual network hydrogel were shown in supporting information.

The microstructure of His-GQDs was investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2010, JEOL). The chemical composition was determined using fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo). The work functions (ΦSA) of GQDs obtained from the literature were 5.08 eV [11], and the BE scale was set at 284.5 eV. Experimental details of the XPS measurements were shown in supporting material [12]. The surface morphology of composite hydrogels was observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-7001F, JEOL). The phase composition was analysed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Dmax-3C, Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å). Raman spectroscopy was performed on a LabRam HR laser confocal Raman spectrum with a laser wavelength of 532 nm.

The antimicrobial activity of the composite hydrogel with different contents of His-GQDs against S. aureus was evaluated by plate counting method. The morphology of the bacteria was observed by SEM. The antimicrobial capacity was further verified by live/dead fluorescence staining. The cytocompatibility was evaluated by live/dead fluorescent staining and cytoskeleton.

The TEM image in Fig. 1 a showed that the His-GQDs were well dispersed. The HR-TEM image demonstrated that the lattice fringe spacing of His-GQDs was 0.198 nm, corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite carbon (Fig. 1b). The FTIR spectrum of the composite hydrogel was shown in Fig. 1c. The characteristic peaks at 3322 cm−1 and 2954 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of COOH and CH2 groups, respectively. The peaks at 1709 cm−1, 1297 cm−1 and 1166 cm−1 were attributed to the characteristic bending vibration of C = O bond, C-N bond and C-O bond, respectively. In addition, a significant absorption peak at 1635 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C = N bond, indicating the presence of imidazole in the edge group of His-GQDs [10]. As shown in Fig. 1S, the peak in the XRD spectrum of His-GQDs could be corresponded to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [13,14]. The Raman spectrum of His-GQDs was presented in Fig. 2 S. The C-N band of imidazole ring was detected at 1218 cm−1 [15], and the band at 1584 cm−1 could be assigned to the C4 = C5 stretching vibration of the imidazole ring.

The XPS survey spectrum shown in Fig. 1d indicated that His-GQDs possessed C, N and O. For the high-resolution spectra of C 1 s (Fig. 1e), three fitting peaks at 284.3, 284.9 and 287.9 eV were attributed to C-C, C-N and C = O bonds, respectively. The peaks at 399.4 and 399.7 eV in the high-resolution spectra of N 1 s corresponded to the presence of pyridinic type and pyrrolic type N atoms (Fig. 1f). The results showed that the imidazole ring had been successfully introduced into the edges of graphene sheets. Fig. 1g showed the high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s. Two separate fitting peaks at 530.5 and 531.4 eV were associated with C = O and C-O bonds, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1h, the His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel possessed a three-dimensional network porous structure.

As shown in Fig. 2a and b, there was no significant distinction in the number of bacterial counts between the G/SA hydrogel and the blank control group, suggesting that the G/SA hydrogel had no bactericidal ability. However, His-GQDs nanoparticles showed distinct inhibitory effect on the growth of S. aureus with antibacterial rate of 96.18 % after 6 h of exposure. The antibacterial activity of His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel increased gradually with the increase of His-GQDs content. Besides the content of His-GQDs, the antibacterial activity also increased gradually with the extended contact time. The antibacterial efficiency of the 0.5 wt% His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel reached 99.99 % after 6 h of contact.

The morphology of S. aureus after treatment with His-GQDs nanoparticles and the composite hydrogels for 6 h was observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 2c, the bacterial cell membranes remained smooth and intact after treatment with G/SA hydrogel. By contrast, the bacteria on the surfaces of the other samples showed different degrees of wrinkled and deformation. In particular, the bacteria on the surface of the 0.5 wt% His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel was most severely damaged.

The live/dead fluorescence staining results of Fig. 2d were used to further investigate the antimicrobial activity of the samples. The G/SA hydrogel group showed strong green fluorescence, indicating that the growth of bacteria was not be influenced. However, clear red fluorescence was identified on 0.5 wt% His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel, suggesting the excellent antibacterial performance. The above experimental results demonstrated that the introduction of His-GQDs imparted excellent antimicrobial property to the dual-network hydrogel.

The antimicrobial mechanism of His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel had two aspects: (1) The His-GQDs released from the hydrogel encapsulated and punctured the cell membrane of the bacteria, causing the efflux of intracellular material [16]. (2) The imidazole molecule attached to His-GQDs could destroy the cell wall components of bacterial by the release of autolytic enzymes, thus leading to bacterial death [17].

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence live/dead staining results indicated that His-GQDs possessed obvious cytotoxicity after 1, 3 and 5 days of incubation. However, the osteoblasts co-cultured with His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel extract grew well with no apparent dead cells. This is due to the encapsulation of the hydrogel avoiding the high concentration of His-GQDs to contact with the osteoblasts. The results of cytoskeleton fluorescence staining are consistent with that of live/dead staining. As in Fig. 3b. the osteoblasts was inhibited significantly in the His-GQDs group, while after co-culture with the extracts of the hydrogels, the cells spread well, indicating that the His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel had good cytocompatibility.

In summary, His-GQDs are successfully prepared by high temperature pyrolysis using citric acid and histidine, and incorporated into G/SA dual network hydrogel. The introduction of His-GQDs significantly improves the antimicrobial property of the hydrogel, and the antimicrobial activity gradually increases with the increase of His-GQDs content and contact time. With His-GQDs content reaches 0.5 wt%, His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network hydrogel is able to completely kill bacteria after 6 h contact with bacteria. The cytotoxicity results demonstrate that encapsulation of His-GQDs in hydrogel is capable of reducing their cytotoxicity and achieving good biocompatibility. His-GQDs/G/SA dual-network antimicrobial hydrogel has promising applications as wound dressings.

Hanyue Hua: Writing – original draft. Jiameng Wang: Data curation. Jien Qin: Project administration. Yongqiang Yang: Funding acquisition. Xiangyu Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52171240) and Natural Science Foundation of Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute of Jiangsu Province (KJ(Y) 2023010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.136651.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
